title: Chapter Thirty-Five: Helen
content: The visitor room at Edinburgh Remand Centre smells like industrial cleaner and old sweat. I'm alone at a metal shelf bolted to the wall, a screen recessed behind scratch-proof glass. The chair is fixed to the floor. Everything here is designed to remind you that you are not in control — not of the room, not of the call, not of the minutes you're allotted.
The screen hums. A low electrical whine that sits just behind my eardrums. I press my palms flat against the cold metal shelf and wait.
Miles appears.

He looks younger. That's the first thing I notice — not that the remand centre has been kind, but that Bly was old. The house aged him. The Game aged him. Without the wainscoting and the long shadows and the authority of that awful place, he's just a boy with deep circles under his eyes, sitting in a grey jumpsuit, hands folded on a grey table. The fluorescence here is unflattering in a different way than Bly's dimness. It shows everything. The acne scars on his jaw. The way his collarbones press against the neckline of the jumpsuit. The slight tremor in his fingers.
He sees me seeing this, and he straightens. Old habit. The posture of the manor.
"Maeve."
"You have information about Helen."
"I do." He glances off-camera — a guard, probably, or a solicitor monitoring the call. Then back to me. "I need you to understand something before I tell you."
"I'm not interested in your preamble, Miles."
"Helen didn't run."

The room is cold. The heating in here is deliberately inadequate — another control mechanism, another way to make visitors want to leave faster. I feel the cold in my knuckles where they press against the shelf, in the tips of my ears, in the breath that fogs slightly between me and the screen.
"What do you mean, she didn't run."
"Mrs. Grose told you she was relocated. That she left abruptly. That she broke, or ran, or went to hospital. None of that's true." He pauses. I watch his throat move. "Helen won the Game."
I say nothing. The hum of the screen fills the silence.

"All seven rounds. She survived. She did what I couldn't do, what Rebecca couldn't do, what Jessel — what no one else managed. She played the Game and she won it." His voice catches on *won*, like the word is a splinter he's been carrying for years. "And then she did what winners do. She wanted the truth."
The cold is in my chest now. Not the room's cold. My own.
"She found Peter Quint's files." I say it flatly. Not a question.

"Just like you did. She found the trust documents. She discovered the whole thing was a construction — the estate, the legacy, all of it. A mechanism for control." He's speaking faster now, the words tumbling out like he's been holding them behind a dam and finally, finally someone gave permission to open the floodgates. "She came to my father with what she'd found. She was calm. She was *reasonable*. She told him she wouldn't sign the NDA, she wouldn't take the settlement, and she was leaving that night."
The taste in my mouth changes. Bile, thin and metallic. I swallow it down.

"She packed her bags," Miles continues. "She called a taxi. She was standing in the front hall with her suitcase when the taxi was called off. Henry intercepted the call. Cancelled it. Told the driver there'd been a mistake."
"So she couldn't leave."

"So she wouldn't leave. Not on her own terms. Not with what she knew." His hands flatten on the table in front of him. The tremor is worse. "My father solved the problem the way he always solved problems. But this time it wasn't an NDA and a cheque."

I want to look away from the screen. I don't. I hold his gaze through the glass and the wire and the distance and I make myself witness this, because whatever he's about to tell me, someone needs to hear it. Someone needs to carry it.

"Helen became a patient," Miles says. "At a private psychiatric facility in Northumberland. Ferndale Clinic. Diagnosed with acute psychotic episode. Committed involuntarily under the Mental Health Act. Her phone was confiscated. Her belongings were stored in a unit my father's solicitor oversaw. Her identity —"
He stops. His jaw works.

"Her identity was replaced," he finishes. "The Helen who came to Bly — the woman with the CV and the references and the National Insurance number — she ceased to exist on paper. She became Patient FN-7754. She's been there for nine years."
The hum of the screen is louder now, or maybe it's the blood in my ears. The cold has spread to my legs. I'm gripping the edge of the shelf so hard my fingertips have gone white.
"She didn't die at Bly," I say. My voice sounds thin. Distant. Like it belongs to someone standing at the far end of a corridor.
"No."
"Helen is alive."
"Yes."
"Locked in a facility for nine years. Drugged. Visited monthly by your father's lawyer to ensure — what? That she stays compliant? That she never tells anyone what she knows?"
Miles closes his eyes. When he opens them, they're wet. Not tears — something rawer than tears. Shame, maybe. The kind of shame that doesn't wash off.

"That's the deal," he says. "That's what I'm telling you. You asked what happened to Helen. This is what happened. This is what my family does. This is what Bly is. It's not a house. It's a mouth. It swallows people and breaks them down and keeps the parts it can use and discards the rest. Helen isn't dead. She's worse than dead. She's been buried alive in paperwork and antipsychotics and a locked ward in Northumberland, and the woman Mrs. Grose thought was *relocated* —"
"Don't," I say. It comes out sharp, and he flinches. "Don't you dare talk about Mrs. Grose's understanding of this. She didn't know. She couldn't have known."

"She knew something was wrong. She told me once, years later, that Helen had seemed too calm when she left. Too quiet. She said it didn't seem right." He shakes his head. "But she didn't ask. She didn't follow up. She did what everyone at Bly does — she accepted the story because the alternative was asking questions she couldn't afford to answer."
I taste bile again. Stronger this time. I breathe through my nose and force it down.
The horror isn't the injustice. I've been living with injustice since I was seventeen years old. The horror is the recognition. The slide-and-click of a lock I've felt on my own door.
This was always the exit from Bly.

Not death. Not the lake, not the road, not the hospital with its clean sheets and its quiet counsellors. The exit was *this* — a room with no handle on the inside, a diagnosis written by someone who'd never examined you, a pharmaceutical fog that turns your mind into a marsh where thoughts sink and vanish before they can reach your mouth. The institutionalization I barely escaped at seventeen — the one I clawed my way out of by sheer luck and fury and a social worker who believed me — that wasn't an aberration. That was the *system*. And Helen has been inside it for nine years.
Nine years.

I try to calculate the hours. I can't. The number collapses under its own weight. Nine years of fluorescent lights and plastic cutlery and supervised phone calls and medication that turns your edges soft until you forget you ever had edges. Nine years of being told you're ill, you're confused, you're a danger to yourself, you need to rest, you need to comply, you need to *calm down*. Nine years of a life that isn't a life — a metabolism, a heartbeat, a set of vital signs — while outside, the world moves on. The seasons change. People forget your name.
This was almost me.

If I'd been two years younger when my mother died. If the institution had kept me the full eighteen months they originally proposed. If I hadn't fought like a feral animal against the soft restraints and the patronising voices and the daily pill that made everything feel like it was happening underwater. If my social worker hadn't answered the phone that night when I called from the ward payphone at 2 a.m., crying so hard I couldn't breathe — if any one of those hinges had swung the other way, I'd be Helen. I'd be FN-7754. I'd be a ghost that breathes.

Helen is the ghost that's not a ghost. She's a living woman buried alive by bureaucracy. Buried by signatures and sections and the quiet, efficient machinery of a man who looked at a problem — a woman who knew too much — and solved it with a phone call to a private clinic and a consultation letter that turned a governess into a patient.

The smell of the industrial cleaner is in my sinuses now. My eyes sting. Not from tears — from the chemicals and the cold and the effort of keeping my face still while something cracks open inside my chest.

"I didn't know they'd do that," Miles says. "I was ten years old when they took Helen. Ten. I remember her standing in the hall with her suitcase. I remember my father coming down the stairs and telling her the taxi had been delayed. I remember going to bed and hearing voices — low, controlled, my father's voice and another voice I didn't recognise. A man's voice. Professional. I thought it was the taxi driver." He exhales. "It was a doctor. They'd already arranged the assessment. They had it planned before Helen even packed her bags. The taxi was never going to come. It was never going to come, Maeve."

His voice breaks on the last sentence. Actually breaks — splinters like wood under pressure. I watch him press the heel of his hand against his eye socket and breathe through his teeth, and I feel nothing for him. Not yet. Maybe not ever. But I don't look away.

"I found out later," he says, steadying himself. "When I was fourteen. I found her file in my father's study. The commitment papers. The quarterly reports from the clinic. Photos — annual assessments. She looked..." He swallows. "She looked like she was drowning in slow motion. Each year a little less there. A little more medicated. A little more gone."
"Go on."

"I didn't say anything." His hands curl into fists on the table. "I need you to understand why. I was afraid they'd do it to Flora. My father had already shown what he was capable of — if I told anyone about Helen, if I exposed the system, Flora would be next. Flora, who was still a child, who still lived in that house, who had no one to protect her except me and Mrs. Grose and the same walls that had swallowed Helen. So I stayed silent. I protected Flora by staying silent. And Helen —"
He can't finish the sentence. I finish it for him.
"Helen stayed buried."
He nods. A single, jerky motion.

"So why now?" I ask. "Why tell me this now? You could have stayed silent. You could have taken the deal Henry offered you — whatever it was — and walked away with your secrets intact. Why this? Why Helen?"

Miles looks at me. Through the screen, through the glass, through the bars and the wire and the distance and the years of silence, he looks at me like I'm the first person who's ever asked him a question he actually has to answer.

"Because I can't be silent anymore," he says. "Because Flora is safe now — she's with our aunt, she's away from Bly, she's *out*. And because I've been carrying this for six years and I can feel it rotting inside me. I watched Jessel die. I filmed it. I *laughed*, Maeve. I laughed while a woman drowned. And I told myself it was the Game, it was the rules, it was what we did at Bly — and some part of me knew, even then, that it was wrong. That I was wrong. But I didn't stop. I didn't stop because stopping would mean admitting everything, and admitting everything would mean losing Flora."
He leans forward. His face fills the screen. The circles under his eyes are so dark they look like bruises.
"Ferndale — Ferndale Clinic, Northumberland. Patient number FN-7754. That's the deal. You get Helen. You find her. You get her out. And I get —"
He stops. His jaw tightens.
"I get a chance to not be my father."

I stare at the screen. The screen stares back. Two faces, both of us trapped behind glass — him in the remand centre, me in the visitor room, Helen in a ward in Northumberland. Three people in three kinds of cage, and the only thing connecting us is a thread of knowledge that tastes like bile and smells like industrial cleaner and hums like an electrical current that never, ever stops.

Miles Ashford. The boy who filmed a woman's death. The boy who laughed. The boy who stayed silent for six years while a living woman rotted in a locked ward because he was afraid the same thing would happen to his sister. He's giving me the key to a cell I've been searching for since I first opened Peter Quint's file. He's handing me the most dangerous piece of information I've ever held — the location of a living witness, a woman who can confirm everything, who can testify, who can bring the whole structure down.
And he's doing it because he wants — what? Absolution? A lighter sentence? The chance to look in the mirror and see something other than his father?
It doesn't matter why. Or maybe it matters, but it doesn't change the fact. He's chosen. After years of silence, after Jessel, after the Game, after everything — he's chosen.
People are not one thing. They're not even the sum of their worst moment. They're the choice they make when they finally get to choose.

Miles chose this. Late. Compromised. Motivated by guilt and fear and self-interest as much as by anything resembling conscience. But he chose it. And Helen is in Northumberland, and she has been there for nine years, and if I don't find her, no one will.
"I'll find her," I say.
Miles exhales. His whole body seems to deflate — not relief, exactly, but something adjacent to it. A loosening. A setting down.
"Thank you," he says. The words are barely audible.

I don't respond to that. I reach for the red button on the console — the one that ends the call, the one that severs the connection, the one that returns me to a room that smells like chemicals and a world that just got darker and more urgent and more personal than it was twenty minutes ago.
My finger hovers over the button.
"Miles."
He looks up.
"It doesn't erase the laughing."
"I know."

I press the button. The screen goes dark. The hum cuts off. The silence rushes in like water filling a cavity, and I'm alone in the cold room with my hands on the metal shelf and my heart doing something it hasn't done in weeks — beating with something other than calculation.
Not strategy. Not the next move in the Game. Not the careful architecture of leverage and exposure.
Purpose.
I stand up. My legs are stiff from the cold, from sitting still, from the weight of what I've just learned. I walk to the door. I press the buzzer for the guard. I wait.
And behind my eyes, behind the plans that are already forming — Northumberland, Ferndale Clinic, FN-7754, a woman who's been buried alive for nine years — behind all of that, a single anxious pulse:
Can I reach her before they know I know?

Because Henry Ashford may be in custody, but the system he built is not. Lawyers can be called. Files can be moved. Patients can be transferred — or worse, their medication can be increased until there's nothing left to rescue. The machinery of Helen's imprisonment didn't stop when Henry's wrists were placed in cuffs. It's still running. It's running right now.

The door buzzes open. I step through. The corridor is long and white and cold, and I walk it like a woman moving towards something that matters more than revenge, more than justice, more than the truth I came to Bly to find.
I walk it like a woman going to dig up the living.
